stories.headline: Frontier Airlines jet strikes pedestrian on runway at Denver International Airport during takeoff
stories.summary:
A Frontier Airlines flight was evacuated late Friday after the crew reported striking a pedestrian on the runway while departing Denver International Airport. Frontier Flight 4345, an Airbus A321 carrying 224 passengers and seven crew members, was bound for Los Angeles when the incident occurred around 11:20 p.m. Pilots aborted the takeoff after smoke was reported in the cabin, and Denver firefighters extinguished a brief engine fire.

Passengers were evacuated via emergency slides and transported by bus to the terminal. The flight was rescheduled to depart Saturday morning. The airport confirmed the affected runway was closed pending investigation.

Frontier said it is investigating the incident in coordination with the airport and other safety authorities. 'We are deeply saddened by this event,' the airline said in a statement. The condition of the person struck on the runway was not immediately disclosed in early reports.

stories.headline: Broncos extend GM George Paton through 2030 season on new five-year contract
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos have signed general manager George Paton to a five-year contract extension that will keep him leading the team's front office through the 2030 season, the club announced Friday. CEO and owner Greg Penner cited Paton's leadership, vision and collaborative partnership with head coach Sean Payton as the driving factors behind the deal.

Paton was originally hired as GM in January 2021 on a six-year contract after serving as the Minnesota Vikings' assistant general manager for nine seasons. The 2026 season would have been the final year of his original deal. According to a source with knowledge of the process, ownership finalized the extension over the past two weeks following the conclusion of the NFL Draft.

Paton had been loosely connected to the Vikings' open general manager position after Minnesota fired Kwesi Adofo-Mensah in January, but sources told The Denver Post he was never seriously in danger of leaving Denver. His relationship with Sean Payton, who wields significant organizational influence, proved central to his continued tenure with the club.

stories.headline: Colorado legislature kills data center tax incentive bill; regulatory measure still in play in final session days
stories.summary:
Colorado lawmakers voluntarily killed House Bill 1030, legislation that would have offered tens of millions of dollars in sales tax breaks to data center developers in exchange for regulatory compliance, as the General Assembly nears its scheduled adjournment next Wednesday. Bill sponsor Rep. Alex Valdez, a Denver Democrat, pulled the measure after it faced mounting opposition, warning that the outcome leaves Colorado at a competitive disadvantage against states like Wyoming and Texas.

A companion regulatory bill, Senate Bill 102, remains alive but has yet to pass a single committee vote. Sponsored by Sen. Cathy Kipp and Rep. Kyle Brown, the measure would impose regulations on large data center development without offering any tax incentives. Its sponsors said Friday that negotiations between competing interests were still ongoing and it remained unclear whether a deal could be reached before session's end.

Separately, the House passed Senate Bill 5 and sent it to Gov. Jared Polis. The measure allows Coloradans to sue federal agents over civil rights violations, but is limited to immigration enforcement contexts. The bill passed on a party-line 41-22 vote. Polis had backed a broader version that died earlier in the session after opposition from prosecutors and local government groups.

stories.headline: Denver Police propose expanding Taser use to lower threat threshold, drawing rebuke from city watchdog
stories.summary:
The Denver Police Department is proposing a change to its use-of-force policy that would lower the threshold required before officers may deploy a Taser, shifting from the current 'active aggression' standard to the lesser 'defensive resistance' standard. Under the proposed revision, officers could use a Taser on someone resisting control or attempting to flee, even without presenting an active threat to officers or others.

The city's Office of the Independent Monitor released a public report this week sharply criticizing the proposal. Independent Monitor Lisabeth Perez Castle said the change 'will put many more community members at risk of harm' and warned it could undermine public trust and expose the city to excessive-force claims. The monitor's office also questioned the scientific basis for DPD's assertion that earlier Taser deployment leads to safer outcomes, noting the only supporting claim found came from Axon, the Taser manufacturer.

DPD defended the proposal, arguing that introducing a Taser during defensive resistance could de-escalate encounters and reduce the likelihood of more serious injury to both officers and civilians. The department also linked the policy revision to its transition from the TASER 7 to the newer TASER 10 model, which operates at significantly lower voltage and features a longer range. The current Taser standard was developed through an extensive community engagement process in 2017 and 2018.

stories.headline: Jeffco Public Schools Superintendent Tracy Dorland to resign this summer, effective July 5
stories.summary:
Jefferson County Public Schools Superintendent Tracy Dorland announced Friday she will resign from leading Colorado's second-largest K-12 district, with her last day set for July 5. Dorland said she is leaving to pursue a new opportunity but did not specify the position. She has led the district since 2021, making her Jeffco's sixth superintendent in seven years at the time of her hiring.

Dorland's tenure was marked by the district's post-pandemic recovery, significant budget cuts and school closures tied to enrollment declines, and the investigation of a high-level administrator for possession of child sexual abuse material. Last year, the district's teachers union voted unanimously 'no confidence' in Dorland, citing concerns over school safety and a lack of transparency in decision-making.

The Board of Education said it will identify an interim superintendent and launch a national search for a permanent replacement. Dorland is the second superintendent to depart one of Colorado's largest school districts in 2026, following Cherry Creek Schools Superintendent Christopher Smith's abrupt resignation in January.

stories.headline: Denver Public Schools projects $28.6 million funding gap over four years, warns of future school closures
stories.summary:
Denver Public Schools is projecting a $28.6 million funding shortfall over the next four years, with district leaders warning that additional school closures are likely as enrollment continues to decline. Superintendent Alex Marrero told the board during a Thursday presentation on the district's proposed $1.5 billion budget for the upcoming school year that closures are expected, though not in the immediate term.

Chief Financial Officer Chuck Carpenter said the district is currently in a sound financial position with a balanced budget, but that will change as enrollment losses intensify and the effects of state 'smoothing' — which averages pupil counts over three years and has temporarily propped up funding — begin to dissipate. District officials also cited uncertain state and federal funding as ongoing risks.

The proposed budget underscores DPS's push for a potential mill levy override on the November ballot, which if approved by voters would generate approximately $43 million annually. The request comes 18 months after district leaders told voters that a nearly $1 billion bond measure in 2024 would not raise taxes. State lawmakers recently closed a $1 billion-plus budget gap through Medicaid cuts and reserve draws, and the governor has signaled a pause in new school finance funding beginning in 2027-28.

stories.headline: New free Westracks shuttle to connect RTD's Golden light rail stop to Red Rocks, Dinosaur Ridge and Morrison
stories.summary:
A new free shuttle service called Westracks will launch Memorial Day weekend, connecting passengers from RTD's W Line light rail terminus at the Jefferson County Government Center in Golden to several popular Jefferson County destinations. The shuttle will stop at Matthews/Winters Park, Dinosaur Ridge, the Red Rocks Trading Post, downtown Morrison and the Morrison Natural History Museum, running approximately every 15 minutes from 9 a.m. to 5 p.m.

The service will operate Saturdays, Sundays and holidays through Labor Day. Jefferson County Parks and Open Space Director Aaron Roth said the goal is to make the region's most iconic landscapes accessible regardless of vehicle ownership while reducing traffic and parking congestion at popular trailheads.

The shuttle addresses longstanding access and parking challenges at high-traffic destinations along the Dakota Ridge corridor. The Red Rocks area in particular has struggled with overcrowded parking lots on weekends, and the new transit link offers an alternative for Denver metro residents who rely on public transportation.

stories.headline: Dardano's Shoes to anchor 18,000-square-foot space at Belcaro Shopping Center redevelopment on Colorado Boulevard
stories.summary:
Denver-based Dardano's, one of the city's longest-running independent shoe retailers, plans to open an 18,000-square-foot store at the redeveloped Belcaro Shopping Center on Colorado Boulevard. The move follows the demolition of a liquor store that previously occupied the site. The new location would represent a significant expansion for the family-owned footwear company.

Dardano's has been a fixture in the Denver retail landscape for decades and the Belcaro deal would give the company a prominent presence along one of the city's busiest commercial corridors. Details on the timeline for construction and an opening date were not immediately available.

The Belcaro Shopping Center redevelopment is part of broader commercial reinvestment activity along the Colorado Boulevard corridor in Denver's southeast quadrant. The project reflects continued demand for anchor tenants in neighborhood retail centers despite broader headwinds facing brick-and-mortar retail.

stories.headline: Michelin-starred Alma Fonda Fina chef Johnny Curiel expands Mexican restaurant empire with new Denver location Milpero
stories.summary:
Chef Johnny Curiel, whose Fonda Fina Hospitality group operates Denver's Michelin-starred Alma Fonda Fina, is opening a new restaurant called Milpero in Denver's River North neighborhood. The new concept expands Curiel's portfolio to six locations nationwide.

Curiel has become one of Denver's most prominent culinary figures, earning a Michelin star for Alma Fonda Fina, which focuses on elevated Mexican cuisine. The River North district has emerged as a hub for notable restaurant openings in recent years, and Milpero adds to that concentration of independent dining concepts.

Further details on Milpero's opening date, menu focus and exact location within the RiNo neighborhood were not immediately available. The expansion underscores continued investment in Denver's dining scene by established local operators despite recent high-profile closures elsewhere in the market.

stories.headline: Avalanche take commanding 2-0 series lead over Wild with dominant Game 2 victory
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche moved to a 2-0 series lead over the Minnesota Wild in their Stanley Cup Playoff series after a 5-2 victory in Game 2, following a high-scoring 9-6 win in the series opener. Nathan MacKinnon recorded a three-point night in Game 2, continuing his dominant postseason play. The two wins give Colorado a strong position heading into the series' next games.

The Avalanche's back-to-back victories demonstrated the team's depth and firepower, with Game 1's offensive outburst and Game 2's more controlled performance both resulting in Colorado wins. The series shifts to Minnesota with the Avalanche holding a significant advantage.

Colorado enters the series as one of the favorites to advance deep into the playoffs. MacKinnon's production has been central to the team's success, and the Avalanche's ability to win in contrasting styles — a shootout and a more disciplined effort — underscores the team's versatility heading into the remainder of the series.

stories.headline: North Fork Valley peach crop largely survives cold snap; harvest expected earlier than normal this season
stories.summary:
A late cold snap damaged peach crops in Colorado's North Fork Valley but left approximately 80 to 90 percent of the harvest intact, according to reporting from the Colorado Sun. The surviving crop is expected to begin appearing at fruit stands in June, earlier than the typical seasonal window.

The North Fork Valley, centered around Hotchkiss and Paonia in Delta County, is one of Colorado's most productive fruit-growing regions and supplies peaches to Front Range markets including the Denver metro area. Early-season cold events pose one of the most consistent threats to the region's orchards, and growers have faced recurring frost damage in recent years.

The earlier-than-normal harvest timeline could benefit consumers and retailers who had anticipated potential shortages following reports of the cold weather event. Full details on the scope of the damage and affected orchards were not immediately available.

stories.headline: Bear attack kills Florida hiker at Glacier National Park; Colorado wildlife officials issue spring wildlife safety reminders
stories.summary:
A Florida man, identified as Anthony Pollio, 33, of Fort Lauderdale, was killed in an apparent bear attack at Montana's Glacier National Park, with his body found May 6 after he was reported missing. The body was discovered about 2.5 miles up the Mt. Brown Trail with injuries consistent with a bear encounter. Multiple trails in the area have been closed as wildlife and law enforcement assess ongoing public safety risks.

Officials have not confirmed whether a black bear or grizzly bear was responsible. The last human-caused bear fatality at Glacier National Park prior to this incident occurred in 1998. The death comes as Colorado Parks and Wildlife issued its own spring wildlife safety reminder, urging residents not to interfere with young wildlife and to take precautions to prevent human-bear interactions.

While Colorado does not have an established grizzly bear population, black bears are common throughout the state and can be aggressive. Wildlife officials advise residents to secure food and trash, and to create distance if a black bear is encountered. The incident is a timely reminder for Colorado hikers and campers as outdoor recreation season ramps up.

weather.notable: Nearly 9 degrees above the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: I don't drink coffee. People look at me like I said I don't breathe air. Like, 'How do you function?' I don't. I'm barely getting by.

topStory.headline: Soil Tests at California Home Indicate Human Remains in Kristin Smart Case
topStory.summary:
San Luis Obispo County Sheriff Ian Parkinson announced Friday that scientific analysis of soil samples taken from a central California property connected to convicted killer Paul Flores indicates that human remains were present at the site, either in the past or currently. Investigators have been executing a search warrant since Wednesday at the Arroyo Grande home of Susan Flores, mother of Paul Flores, who was convicted in 2022 of murdering 19-year-old college student Kristin Smart.

Parkinson told reporters that compound analysis of soil samples points to the presence of a decomposing human body, though he stopped short of confirming the remains belong to Smart. "We believe that based on what we're looking at evidence-wise — scientific evidence — that a human's remains were there at one time — or still there. We can't call it Kristin, but there's evidence to support human remains," Parkinson said. He added that investigators had not yet recovered Smart's remains and that the search would continue, with additional soil sampling and ground-penetrating radar equipment being deployed on the property.

Smart disappeared on May 25, 1996, after attending an off-campus party while a student at California Polytechnic State University in San Luis Obispo. Prosecutors alleged she was killed during an attempted rape by Paul Flores, who was the last known person to see her. Smart was declared legally dead in 2002, and her body has never been located despite decades of investigation.

The current search is not the first time investigators have examined properties tied to the Flores family. Archaeologists previously searched the home of Paul Flores' father, Ruben Flores, and found a soil disturbance described as roughly the size of a casket along with human blood too degraded to yield usable DNA. Ruben Flores was charged as an accessory after the fact for allegedly helping his son bury and later move Smart's remains, but he was acquitted by a jury in 2022. Susan Flores has not been charged with any crime in connection with the case.

Sheriff Parkinson said investigators do not yet know how long the search of the property will continue and have not confirmed whether they will seek an additional warrant to begin excavation. The case drew widespread public attention in part because of podcast coverage that aided investigators in building the case that ultimately led to Paul Flores' first-degree murder conviction. Authorities said their goal remains to bring Smart's remains home to her family.
